items.q: Why was the monarchy restored in 1660?
items.correct: After Cromwell's death the Protectorate collapsed; General Monck negotiated Charles II's return on the terms of the Declaration of Breda (amnesty, religious flexibility, army pay).
items.misconception: Charles II invaded England with a French army and seized the throne.
items.random_wrong: Parliament voted to restore the monarchy because Cromwell had taxed them too heavily.
items.why: Charles returned by invitation, not invasion. Students imagine a violent reconquest; in fact, the political class restored him to escape military rule.

items.q: Why did the superpowers pursue détente in the 1970s?
items.correct: Mutual recognition of nuclear parity, US exhaustion from Vietnam, Soviet economic stagnation, and the cost of the arms race — formalised in SALT and the Helsinki Accords.
items.misconception: Détente ended the Cold War in the 1970s.
items.random_wrong: Détente meant the USSR converted to capitalism.
items.why: Détente managed rivalry, it did not end it; the Cold War froze again under Reagan. Students mistake reduced tension for resolution.

items.q: Why is Magna Carta still cited in 'Power and the People' history?
items.correct: Because clauses on lawful judgement and protection from arbitrary imprisonment were reinterpreted in the 17th century to support broader liberties, even though they originally protected only barons.
items.misconception: Magna Carta directly created modern human rights in 1215.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta gave the right to vote to all English men.
items.why: Its symbolic power grew over time, especially during the 17th-century constitutional struggles. Students confuse historical use with original meaning — a key historiographical insight.

items.q: Which 1954 Supreme Court case desegregated public schools?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Brown v. Board of Education. “Plessy v. Ferguson” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Whose December 1955 arrest sparked the Montgomery Bus Boycott?
items.why: Correct answer: Rosa Parks. “Coretta Scott King” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In which year was Martin Luther King Jr. assassinated in Memphis?
items.why: The real figure is 1968. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
